note: Plain-language help for people facing a civil lawsuit without a lawyer (pro se / self-represented). This is general information and official resources — NOT legal advice. For advice about your specific case, talk to a lawyer or a free legal-aid office.

firstSteps.p: If you do nothing, the court can enter a default judgment against you — the other side wins automatically and can garnish wages or freeze accounts. Acting fast is your strongest move.
Find three things: WHO is suing you, WHAT they claim you did, and your DEADLINE to respond (often 20–30 days from when you were served). Circle that date.

An 'Answer' is your written response — you admit or deny each claim and raise any defenses. File it with the court clerk before the deadline, send a copy to the other side, and keep a stamped copy for yourself.
Collect anything that tells your side: contracts, receipts, bank records, texts, emails, photos, names of witnesses. Organize it by date.

firstSteps.h: Get free help — you don't have to do this alone
firstSteps.p: Court Help Centers, legal-aid offices, and DIY form tools (below) are free. Many courts have a self-help desk right in the building.

firstSteps.h: Show up to every court date
firstSteps.p: Always appear (or ask the court about appearing remotely). Bring your documents, dress neatly, be respectful, and tell your story with your evidence.

items.name: Find legal aid near you (LSC)
items.what: Locator for free civil legal-aid offices funded by the Legal Services Corporation.

items.name: LawHelp.org
items.what: Free legal information and a directory of local legal-aid programs by state and topic.

items.name: LawHelpNY
items.what: Free legal help, plain-language guides, and a legal-aid finder for New Yorkers.

items.name: The Legal Aid Society (NYC)
items.what: Free legal representation and advice for low-income New Yorkers in civil cases.

items.name: NY CourtHelp — DIY Forms
items.what: Free guided programs that build your court papers (including an Answer in a consumer-debt case) by asking plain questions.

items.name: NY CourtHelp
items.what: Step-by-step guides for common civil problems: being sued, small claims, housing, family.

items.name: Federal court forms
items.what: Official national forms for federal court, including pro se complaint and motion templates.

items.name: Small claims (NYC)
items.what: How to sue or defend yourself for smaller amounts — simpler, lawyer-optional court.

items.name: Representing Yourself (U.S. Courts)
items.what: The federal judiciary's overview of going to court without a lawyer.

items.name: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure
items.what: The actual rules that govern civil lawsuits in federal court (free, via Cornell).

items.name: Pro Se (self-represented) resources — SDNY
items.what: Example of a district court's pro se manual, intake office, and filing help.

items.name: How courts connect (Pocket Politics)
items.what: Our visual map of which court hears what, and how a case moves up on appeal.
